structure.title: of 185 Document ID 1098: AUTOSAR_AP_SWS_RawDataStream
structure.summary: The section details the **IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer** class, which includes special member functions (copy constructor, default constructor, move constructor, copy assignment operator, move assignment operator, and destructor) and member functions like **Connect**, **Shutdown**, and **WriteData**. It also outlines named constructors (e.g., **Create**) for object instantiation. The **rds_error_domain.h** header

structure.title: Demands and constraints on Base Software (normative) 170
History of Constraints and Specification Items 173
Introduction and functional overview
structure.summary: This specification defines the functionality, API, and configuration for the `Raw Data Stream` functional cluster in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform, enabling applications to process raw binary data streams. It addresses two scenarios: (1) continuous byte streams where data is untyped and requires no serialization, and (2) Ethernet datagram streams, where headers are typed data and payloads are binary data. The cluster provides an interface to handle these streams, with the term `Raw Data Stream` representing a binary data stream processed as either a continuous byte sequence or atomic datagram units. The specification ensures compatibility with both serialized and untyped data handling in communication channels.

structure.title: Raw Data Streaming interface is statically defined and dependent on the underlying network protocol. The modeling for the Raw Data Streaming Interface supports the following network protocols:
structure.summary: The Raw Data Streaming interface is statically defined and protocol-dependent, supporting two network protocols: IP-based over Ethernet (network layer) and IEEE1722 (data link layer). For IP-based streams, TCP/IP sockets (transport layer) use TCP or UDP, with TCP preferred for reliability and UDP for unreliable scenarios, while secure communication employs TLS/IPSec and IAM access control. IEEE1722 streams utilize data link sockets (ISO OSI layer 2) with MAC unicast/m

structure.title: Acronyms and Abbreviations
structure.summary: The section provides a glossary of acronyms and abbreviations specific to this specification. It notes that these terms are only relevant within the context of the current document. A general list of acronyms and abbreviations is referenced in [2]. The glossary does not include terms from external documents or standards. All listed abbreviations are defined for use within this specification.

nodes.title: MAC unicast socket connection
nodes.summary: A MAC unicast socket connection is a communication method using a socket provided by the operating system, bound to the data link layer (ISO OSI Layer 2). It employs a media access control (MAC) address as the destination identifier, ensuring communication with exactly one specific device (unicast). The MAC address operates at the data link layer, enabling direct hardware-level addressing. This connection type is restricted to a single recipient, as unicast communication does not support broadcasting or multicasting. The binding to Layer 2 ensures low-level, direct data transmission between devices.

nodes.title: MAC multicast socket connection
nodes.summary: A "MAC multicast socket connection" refers to a socket-based communication method bound to the data link layer (ISO OSI Layer 2), utilizing a media access control (MAC) address. This allows the socket to multicast data to one or more destination devices sharing the same MAC address. The connection leverages the data link layer for direct hardware-level communication, enabling efficient broadcast-like transmission within a local network. It is distinct from higher-layer multicast protocols as it operates at the physical layer interface. The MAC address serves as the target identifier for simultaneous communication with multiple recipients.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-stream-header
nodes.summary: The "AVTPDU-common-stream-header" is defined by IEEE 1722 and extends the AVTPDU-common-header, which is utilized by specific AVTP stream data subtypes. It is designed for a subset of AVTP stream data subtypes, including 61883_IIDC, AAF, TSCF, and RVF. This header provides additional fields or functionality to support the unique requirements of these subtypes within the AVTP protocol framework. The expansion ensures compatibility and structured data transmission for these specialized stream types. The definition explicitly references IEEE 1722 as the standard source for this header structure.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-alternative-header
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-alternative-header is defined by [1, IEEE1722] and serves AVTP stream data subtypes that lack the commonalities shared by formats using AVTPDU-common-stream-header or AVTPDU-common-control-headers. It is specifically employed for subtypes like CRF and NTSCF, which do not conform to the standard headers used for other AVTP data formats. This header provides an alternative structure to accommodate distinct data formatting requirements of these subtypes. The specification ensures compatibility while allowing flexibility for diverse AVTP stream implementations. The text explicitly identifies CRF and NTSCF as examples of subtypes utilizing this header.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-payload
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-payload is the second component of an AVTPDU, as defined by [1, IEEE1722], and is responsible for carrying data encoded in the AVTPDU-header's subtype. It transports payload content specific to the subtype, such as audio samples in the case of an AAF-payload. The payload structure is determined by the subtype field in the AVTPDU-header, ensuring data alignment with the intended media type. This mechanism enables efficient transmission of audio and video data within the AVTPDU framework. The example illustrates how subtypes direct the payload's content, such as audio samples for AAF-payloads.

nodes.title: Stream data producer
nodes.summary: A "stream data producer" is an end node in an Ethernet network that continuously generates data transmitted via a stream. This data is received by one or multiple end nodes acting as stream data consumers. The term "talker" is used interchangeably with "stream data producer" to describe this role. The producer's function is central to enabling data flow in Ethernet-based streaming applications. The definition emphasizes continuous, unidirectional data transmission without additional context.

nodes.title: Stream data consumer
nodes.summary: A "stream data consumer" is an end node in an Ethernet network that continuously consumes data. It receives data through a stream, indicating ongoing, real-time data transmission. The term emphasizes the node's role as a recipient of continuous data flow rather than discrete packets. This definition aligns with its function in Ethernet-based streaming applications. The consumer operates as the final destination for the streamed data.

nodes.title: AVTP presentation time
nodes.summary: The AVTP presentation time, as defined in [1, IEEE1722], is the gPTP time when data within an AVTPDU payload is delivered to a time-sensitive application. It is encoded in the "avtp_timestamp" field of the AVTPDU-common-stream-header. The time is calculated as TavtpPresentationTime = TcurrentGlobalTime + TmaxTransitTime. It explicitly excludes format conversion and processing delays of the receiving application, as illustrated in IEEE1722 Figure 6.

nodes.title: Max transit time
nodes.summary: The "Max transit time" is defined by [1, IEEE1722] and is calculated as the worst-case transit time from a stream data producer (talker) to a stream data consumer (listener). The method involves selecting a max transit time that is greater than or equal to the largest worst-case transit time observed in the system. This ensures the chosen value accommodates the maximum possible delay in data transmission between producers and consumers. The definition emphasizes the need to account for the worst-case scenario to guarantee reliable stream data delivery. No additional parameters or exceptions are specified in the text.

nodes.title: Media clock
nodes.summary: The "Media clock" is defined by [1, IEEE1722] as an entity that generates a rate, such as a precise hardware clock operating at a constant rate (e.g., 48kHz). It serves as a timing reference for media synchronization. The media clock is hosted by the media clock provider, which manages its operation and ensures consistent timing across systems. The definition emphasizes its role in maintaining accurate temporal alignment for media processing. The example of 48kHz illustrates a typical application of a constant-rate clock in audio or multimedia contexts.

nodes.title: Media clock provider
nodes.summary: A media clock provider is an end node hosting a media clock that transmits an IEEE1722 stream of subtype CRF to one or more media clock consumers. The stream includes presentation timestamps aligned with the media clock rate, enabling synchronization. The term "listener" is used interchangeably with "stream data consumer" in this context. The CRF stream serves as a clock reference format, ensuring temporal coordination between nodes. The provider's role is critical for maintaining timing accuracy in IEEE1722-based networks.

nodes.title: Media clock consumer
nodes.summary: A "media clock consumer" is an end node receiving IEEE1722 streams of subtype CRF (clock reference format) from a media clock provider, using the encapsulated data to recover its media clock (e.g., PLL). It relies on the provider's CRF stream for synchronization. An "ACF-stream" refers to IEEE1722 streams of subtype TSCF (time-synchronous control format) or NTSCF (non-time-s

nodes.title: ACF-message
nodes.summary: The ACF-message, as defined in IEEE1722, encapsulates a bus frame of a specific type (e.g., CAN) using an associated ACF-message type (e.g., ACF_CAN). It comprises an ACF-message-header and an ACF-message-payload. Multiple ACF-messages of varying types can aggregate into an ACF-payload, which is transmitted within a single ACF-stream. The encapsulation ensures compatibility with different bus protocols while enabling structured data transport. The structure preserves technical specifics of the underlying bus frame and its associated message type.

nodes.title: ACF-message-payload
nodes.summary: The "ACF-message-payload" is defined by [1, IEEE1722] and constitutes the second part of an ACF-message. It carries data corresponding to the ACF-message type, which is encoded in the ACF-message-header. For instance, an ACF_CAN-payload specifically transports a CAN2.0 frame.

nodes.title: Input documents & related standards and norms
Further applicable specification
nodes.summary: The functional cluster adheres to AUTOSAR's core specification [3], with chapter 7.1 ("General requirements for all Functional Clusters") serving as an additional required specification. For the **Raw Data Stream** functional cluster, AUTOSAR's general specification [4, RS General] applies, and the SWS General specification is also required. Specific requirements for the **Raw Data Stream** functional cluster are detailed in [5, RS Communication Management]. All referenced specifications are mandatory for implementation.

nodes.title: Known limitations
nodes.summary: The current solution lacks support for runtime network topology changes, such as service discovery, requiring RawDataStreams to be statically configured on the same ECU

structure.title: A **Raw Data Streams** using IEEE1722 protocol support the following IEEE1722 subtypes (specified by [1, IEEE1722]) as transport protocol: **AAF**, **61883_IIDC**, **RVF**, **CRF**, **TSCF** and NTSCF**. Support of other subtypes specified by [1, IEEE1722] may be added in future.
Dependencies to other Functional Clusters
structure.summary: This chapter outlines the dependencies of the functional cluster on other functional clusters, emphasizing that AUTOSAR does not standardize interfaces between them to enable efficient, OS-dependent implementations. The chapter provides an informative guideline for interactions without specifying syntactical details, ensuring compatibility between documents and supporting parallel development of functional clusters. Internal interfaces, parameters, and return values are left to platform providers, with the possibility of additional details being added. A comprehensive technical architecture documentation for the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform is referenced in [6].

nodes.title: Provided Interfaces
nodes.summary: The "Provided Interfaces" section outlines the public interfaces available from this functional cluster to other functional clusters. It serves as an overview of the APIs or communication protocols exposed by the cluster for interaction with external components. The interfaces are designed to enable interoperability and data exchange between functional clusters within the system. No specific interface details, purposes, or implementation mechanisms are described in this summary. The focus remains on the scope of interfaces provided rather than their functional characteristics.

nodes.title: Required Interfaces
nodes.summary: The "Required Interfaces" section outlines the public interfaces needed by the Raw Data Stream functional cluster from other clusters in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. It specifies three required interfaces: **CryptoStack** (from Cryptography) for encrypted connections, **Logger** (from Log and Trace) for standardized message logging, and **SynchronizedTimeBaseConsumer** (from Time Synchronization) for IEEE1722-based communication timing. These interfaces are detailed in Figure 5.1 and Table 5.2, with their respective purposes described. The initial table in the section is empty, indicating no interfaces are provided by the Raw Data Stream cluster itself.

nodes.title: Raw Data Streaming using IP based protocols (network layer)
Raw Data Streaming Interface
nodes.summary: The Raw Data Streaming Interface operates synchronously with blocking behavior by default, but timeout handling is required to return errors if operations exceed specified time limits, which are defined as parameters for each operation. Configuration involves specifying credentials and socket parameters via RawDataStreamMapping and EthernetRawDataStreamMapping, as detailed in TPS_ManifestSpecification [8]. Secure communication

nodes.title: Use cases
nodes.summary: The RawDataStream interface supports three use cases: (1) client connecting to a non-AUTOSAR sensor via socket, (2) server awaiting a connection from a non-AUTOSAR sensor, and (3) client/server communication with another AUTOSAR RawDataStream instance. Socket connections can use UDP or TCP protocols with Unicast or Multicast configurations. These use cases are detailed in Figure 7.2, which specifies the required Deployment model artifacts for configuration. Ethernet socket parameters for these scenarios are described in Chapter 10.

nodes.title: Raw Data Streaming
nodes.summary: The **RawDataStream** C++ API (chapter 8.2) enables IP-based raw data streaming with operations tailored for client and server communication: clients use **Connect**, **Shutdown**, **ReadData**, and **WriteData**, while servers use **WaitFor

nodes.title: Access Control via IAM
nodes.summary: The section outlines restrictions on using RawDataStreams, emphasizing timeout handling for Connect, WaitForConnection, Read, and Write operations. A timeout parameter can be specified; if unspecified, operations block until completion. If a timeout is exceeded, the error code **RdsErrcc::kCommunicationTime-out** is returned, and the connection state is reverted to its pre-operation state. The **RawDataStreamClient::Create()** and **RawDataStreamServer::Create()** constructors validate the InstanceSpecifier to ensure it references a RPortPrototype mapped to the requesting process in the role process, otherwise treating it as a violation.

nodes.title: Secure Communication
nodes.summary: Raw data streams can be transmitted via TCP and UDP, enabling the use of security mechanisms to protect communication. The supported security protocols include TLS 1.2 (RFC5246), DTLS 1.2 (RFC6347), and IPSec. These protocols provide encrypted and authenticated communication channels for securing data transfer over the respective transport protocols.

nodes.title: Creation and use of secure channels for Raw Data Streaming

structure.title: A (D)TLS secure channel may provide authenticity, integrity and confidentiality which may be used with raw data streaming.
structure.summary: The TLS and DTLS implementations must support specific cipher suites: TLS_PSK_WITH_NULL_SHA256 for authentic communication (RFC5487) and TLS_PSK_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 for confidential communication (RFC5487). A TLS secure channel is required if a `tlsSecureComProps` instance is part of an `EthernetRawDataStreamMapping` configured for TCP transmission via a localTcpPort. A DTLS secure channel is required if the same instance is configured for UDP transmission via a localUdpPort. Additionally, IPsec secure channels must comply with [SWS_CM_90117] and [SWS_CM_9

nodes.title: Raw Data Stream Interfaces
nodes.summary: The "Raw Data Stream Interfaces" section outlines the technical specifications for raw data stream handling but does not provide detailed descriptions or implementation specifics. It references chapter 7.1.1.1 for information on supported use cases, indicating that further details about application scenarios are located there. The section itself focuses on interface definitions without elaborating on their functionality or parameters. No additional technical details beyond the reference are included. The summary emphasizes the section's role in directing readers

structure.title: A socket shall be created and bound to the address and port specified in the local credentials. In case of TCP it shall also mark the socket as passive and listen for connections (for use case 1:1 TCP unicast).
structure.summary: The section describes socket creation and configuration based on server credentials. For TCP, a socket is created, bound to the local address/port, marked as passive, and set to listen for connections. For UDP, Remote Unicast Credentials create a unicast endpoint stored in the **Raw-DataStreamServer** object for Read/Write operations. Multicast Credentials bind and join the specified multicast address/port, with the endpoint used for 1:N writes via `WriteData()`; otherwise, the unicast endpoint is used for 1:1 communication.

nodes.title: Set up a RawDataStream connection
nodes.summary: The **RawDataStreamClient::Connect()** function establishes a unicast TCP socket connection to a server, using the manifest's socket endpoints and attributes via the InstanceSpecifier, and initializes TLS/DTLS if configured. For UDP, no connection is established, and packet communication is restricted to the specified address. The **RawDataStreamServer::WaitForConnection()** function prepares the server to accept incoming TCP connections, with the constructor marking the socket for acceptance, while UDP returns immediately without establishing a connection. Both functions adhere to the upstream requirements RS_CM_00410, RS_CM_00411, RS_CM_00412.

nodes.title: Shutdown of a RawDataStream connection
nodes.summary: The `RawDataStreamClient::ShutDown()` and `RawDataStreamServer::ShutDown()` functions close the socket connection for their respective `RawDataStream` instances. Both functions terminate the receiving and sending parts of the socket connection. For TCP, the

nodes.title: Read data from a RawDataStream connection
nodes.summary: The `RawDataStreamClient::ReadData()` function reads data from either a multicast socket (if Multicast Credentials are defined) or a unicast TCP/UDP socket, depending on the connection type (1:N vs. 1:1). It leverages zero-copy semantics via `std::unique_ptr` to transfer data ownership to the application through `ReadDataResult.data`. The `RawDataStreamServer::ReadData()` function similarly reads data from a unicast socket, using the same zero-copy mechanism for efficiency. Both functions are governed by upstream requirements RS_CM_00410, RS_CM_00411, and RS_CM_00412. The data ownership transfer ensures minimal memory duplication and direct access

nodes.title: Write data to a RawDataStream connection
nodes.summary: The `RawDataStreamClient::WriteData()` function writes bytes to the socket connection for a defined RawDataStream instance. If multicast credentials are defined for the client,

structure.title: Raw Data Streaming using IEEE1722 protocol (data link layer)

nodes.title: Raw Data Streaming Interface
nodes.summary: The Raw Data Streaming Interface operates via IEEE1722 protocol with synchronous, blocking operation calls. Configuration involves specifying data link layer socket parameters for IEEE1722 streams using **RawDataStreamMapping** and **EthernetMacRawDataStreamMapping**, as detailed in the _TPS_ManifestSpecification_ [8]. Security is enabled through MACSec in middleware and IAM access control, both configurable in the deployment and mapping model. Applications can act as either **IEEE1722 stream consumers** or **producers**, with Figure 7.3 illustrating the logical usage of Raw Data Streaming

nodes.title: Raw Data Streaming
nodes.summary: The **IEEE1722RawDataStream** communication is statically configured via the Deployment model and operates statelessly in a unidirectional manner

nodes.title: IEEE1722RawDataPredducer using IEEE1722 protocol
nodes.summary: The IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer instance derives its IEEE1722 configuration from the referenced IEEE1722TpConnection, with member variables initialized to its configuration values. If the EthernetConnector's maximum transmission unit (MTU), destination MAC address

nodes.title: Secure Communication
nodes.summary: IEEE1722RawDataStream communication can be secured using the MACSec protocol at the data link layer. This mechanism is outside the scope of the Raw Data Stream functional cluster. The system must establish and configure MACSec independently to ensure secure communication.

nodes.title: Produce IEEE1722 stream data
nodes.summary: To produce IEEE1722 stream data, an application must create an `IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer` instance using the template class `ara::rds::IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer`, specifying the IEEE1722 subtype. This instance represents the configuration for a specific stream, derived into its

structure.title: of 185 Document ID 1098: AUTOSAR_AP_SWS_RawDataStream
structure.summary: The IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer processes datagrams provided as `ara::core::Vector` elements, where each datagram's IEEE1722 header and payload are constructed based on the configured subtype. Mandatory header fields are validated against application-provided values; if missing or invalid, the datagram is skipped. Optional fields use configured or default values if available, otherwise skipped. If mandatory fields exceed valid ranges, the datagram is also skipped

nodes.title: AVTPDU-header creation
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-header creation ensures consistency and completeness of field values provided by the application against the **IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer** configuration during the **WriteData** operation. It adheres to the **IEEE1722**-specified formats, including AVTPDU-common-header, DVTPDU-common-stream-header, DVTPDU-common-control-header, and AVTPDU-alternative-header. Shared fields like stream ID are detailed in Chapter 7.2.4.2.1.1, while subsequent sub-chapters outline specific field settings for each header type. The **AV

nodes.title: Treatment of shared AVTPDU-header fields
nodes.summary: The IEEE1722RawDataStreamConsumer or Producer must maintain separate sequence numbers for IEEE1722 streams with AVTP common-stream-header, CRF, or NTSCF subtypes, incrementing each header creation by 0x01 and wrapping to 0x00 upon reaching the maximum value. When creating AVTPDU headers, the MAC address and unique ID are prioritized from application-provided valid values, or fallback to configured values from IEEE1722TpConnection or mappings. The GlobalTimeDomain's

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-stream-header fields
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-common-stream-header fields are defined by specific rules for IEEE1722 streams. The **mr** (media clock restart) field is set to the application-provided value if valid; otherwise, it defaults to zero. The **sv** (stream_id valid) field is always set to 1. The **sequence_num** is assigned the current value, **tu** (timestamp uncertain) is determined via [SWS_RDS_10548], and **

nodes.title: AVTPDU-alternative-header fields
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-alternative-header fields are specific to AVTP stream data subtypes and are detailed in subchapters dedicated to the IEEE1722 stream for the **CRF** and **NTSCF** subtypes. These fields are defined within the respective subchapters of the IEEE1722 standard, ensuring subtype-specific formatting for AVTP stream data. The description applies exclusively to the **CRF** and **NTSCF** subtypes, as outlined in their corresponding subchapters. No additional fields or details are provided beyond these specifications.

nodes.summary: The section outlines the creation of AVTPDU headers for the 61883_IIDC stream data subtype, specifying header field values based on

nodes.title: AAF-header fields
nodes.summary: The section outlines the configuration of AVTPDU-header fields for IEEE1722 streams with AVTP stream data subtype "AAF," referencing IEEE1722 Chapter 7.2 and 7.3. When the format field indicates AAF, the process sets the **format**, **sp**, and **evt** fields to values from the **IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer** instance (SWS_RDS_90262–90264). For

nodes.title: ACF-header fields
nodes.summary: The section outlines the creation of AVTP headers for ACF (AVTP Control Format) data subtypes NTSCF and TSCF, per IEEE1722 standards. AUTOSAR does not support SRP, but the sv (stream_id valid) field is always set to 0x01. For NTSCF, the AVTPDU header requires setting sv to

nodes.title: CRF-header fields
nodes.summary: The "CRF-header fields" section outlines the requirements for setting specific AVTPDU-header fields when creating an IEEE1722 stream with CRF (Clock Reference Format) subtype, as defined in IEEE1722 chapter 10.4. Key fields include sv, mr, fs, tu, sequence_num, type, stream_id, pull, base_frequency, crf_data_length, timestamp_interval, and crf_data, each set based

structure.summary: The section specifies that fields from IEEE1722 chapter "10.4.13 crf_data field

nodes.title: RVF-header fields
nodes.summary: The "RVF-header fields" section outlines the requirements for setting specific header fields in AVTPDU headers for IEEE1722 streams with RVF subtype, as defined in IEEE1722 chapter 12.2. Fields like active_pixels, total_lines, ap, f, ef, evt, pd, i, pixel_depth, pixel_format, frame_rate, colorspace, num_lines, i_seq_num, and line_number must be populated either by application-provided values or configured values from the IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer instance, with defaults

nodes.title: Error handling for consuming or producing IEEE1722 stream data
IEEE1722RawDataStream
nodes.summary: The IEEE1722RawDataStream section outlines two key behaviors for handling data operations under specific conditions. If a ReadData or WriteData operation is invoked while the data link layer socket is disconnected, the operation must do nothing and return `kStreamAlreadyDisconnected`. Additionally, if an operation is interrupted by the system, it must reset internal states, release resources, and return `kInterruptedBySignal`. Both scenarios are part of a DRAFT specification tied to upstream requirement RS_CM_00413. The status and requirements are repeated for emphasis, ensuring consistency in implementation.

nodes.title: Startup
nodes.summary: The Raw Data Stream functional cluster requires no special startup handling. No state is maintained across power cycles, meaning the system does not retain prior operational context or data after a power interruption. This design ensures minimal initialization overhead and independence between sessions. The absence of state persistence simplifies recovery and eliminates dependencies on prior execution states. Operational continuity is achieved without requiring explicit startup procedures or data reconciliation.

nodes.title: Shutdown
nodes.summary: The "Shutdown" section states that no special shutdown handling is required for the Raw Data Stream functional cluster. Standard operational procedures suffice for terminating its functionality, as no unique or additional steps are mandated during shutdown. This implies the cluster can be gracefully stopped without specific configuration or intervention beyond typical system shutdown practices.

nodes.title: Security Events
nodes.summary: The "Security Events" section explicitly states that the functional cluster does not define any security events. No security-related event configurations, triggers, or definitions are included in this cluster. The absence of security events implies

nodes.title: Log Messages
nodes.summary: The "Log Messages" section states that the functional cluster does not include any non-verbose log messages. Non-verbose log messages refer to modelled DLT (Diagnostic Log Transfer) messages. This indicates the cluster's logging scope excludes standardized diagnostic logging protocols. The absence of such messages suggests a focus on verbose or higher-level logging mechanisms. No additional log message definitions are provided in this context.

nodes.title: Production Errors
nodes.summary: The "Production Errors" section states that the functional cluster does not define any production errors (Diagnostic Events). It specifies a message ID of 0x80001ff9 with a message type of DLT_LOG_FATAL. The associated arguments include: **modeledProcess ld** (UTF-8 encoded uint8, meta-model identifier of the violating process), **location** (UTF-8 encoded uint8, implementation-defined identifier of the violation source), **instanceSpecifier** (UTF-8 encoded uint8, used to create the object), and **className** (UTF-8 encoded uint8, name of the instantiated class). All arguments are described in detail with their respective data types and encoding.

APIs are detailed in tables within subsequent chapters, with 8.1 explaining the structure and content of these tables. The chapter outlines how API descriptions are organized, emphasizing the use of tabular formats for clarity. Technical details about API endpoints, parameters, and operations are referenced in the specified sections. No additional information beyond the described structure is included.

It emphasizes that **Errors** may include vendor-specific codes, and **Violations** or **Exceptions** are listed for compliance. The **

nodes.title: Class: RawDataStreamClient
nodes.summary: The `RawDataStreamClient` class, defined in the `ara::rds` namespace and included via `raw_data_stream.h`, implements the `RawDataStreamClientInterface` for reading/writing binary data streams over network connections. It is a final class with a `Create` function specified in SWS_RDS_10482. The class is tied to the IEEE1722RawDataStreamConsumerInterface and ProducerInterface via their respective API definitions (SWS_RDS_90311, 90312, 90322, 90323). It relies on the `RawDataStreamClientInterface` and associated API functions for its functionality, as outlined in S

nodes.title: Copy Constructor
nodes.summary: The "Copy Constructor" section is not provided in the given text, and no technical details or content are available for summarization. The only information included is a list of upstream requirements (RS_CM_00410, RS_CM_00411, RS_CM_00412, RS_AP_00145, RS_AP_00147) without any explanation or context related to a copy constructor. No further details about its purpose, implementation, or constraints are present.

nodes.title: Move Constructor
nodes.summary: The move constructor for `RawDataStreamClient` is declared as `RawDataStreamClient(RawDataStreamClient &&) noexcept`, taking an rvalue reference to another `RawDataStreamClient` object. It is exception-safe and not thread-safe. The constructor is defined within the `ara::rds::RawDataStreamClient` class in the header file `"ara/rds/raw_data_stream.h"`. Unlike the copy constructor, which is deleted, the move constructor enables transfer of resources from a temporary object. The move constructor adheres to the specified upstream requirements for exception safety and resource management.

nodes.title: Destructor
nodes.summary: The "Destructor" section lists upstream requirements but contains no technical details about the destructor's implementation, purpose, or behavior. It references RS_CM_00410, RS_CM_00411, RS_CM_00412, and RS_AP_00145 as dependencies but does not describe the destructor's functionality, lifecycle, or interaction with other components. The text is purely a reference to compliance standards and lacks any explanatory content about the destructor itself. No technical specifications or code examples are provided. The summary cannot include additional information beyond the given text.

nodes.title: Connect(std::chrono::milliseconds)
nodes.summary: The `Connect(std::chrono::milliseconds timeout)` function establishes a unicast socket connection for the `RawDataStream` instance, connecting to a TCP server within the specified timeout. For UDP, no connection is established. It returns `ara::core::Result<void>` indicating success or an error code. The function is not thread-safe and guarantees exception safety. Errors include `

nodes.title: ReadData(std::size_t, std::chrono::milliseconds)
nodes.summary: The `ReadData` function accepts two parameters: a `std::size_t

nodes.title: ReadData(std::size_t)
nodes.summary: The `ReadData(std::size_t)` function provides two overloads for reading data from a raw data stream. The first overload includes a `std::chrono::milliseconds timeout` parameter for non-blocking operations, while the second omits the timeout. Both return an `ara::core::Result<ReadDataResult>` struct on success or an error code. The function requires a `maxLength` parameter specifying the maximum bytes to read. Errors include `RdsErrc::kStreamNotConnected`, `RdsErrc::kCommunicationTimeout`, and `RdsErrc::kInterruptedBySignal`, with varying rollback semantics. The method is exception-safe but not thread-safe.

nodes.title: Class: RawDataStreamServer
nodes.summary: The `RawDataStreamServer` class, defined in the `ara::rds` namespace, provides a network-based interface for reading and writing binary data streams. It requires an instance specifier to qualify network binding and includes headers `ara/rds/raw_data_stream.h` and `ara/rds/rds_fwd.h`. The class implements the `RawDataStreamServerInterface` and is final, with a constructor that accepts parameters for network configuration. Key violations include address unavailability, invalid instance specifiers, mismatched port interfaces, and duplicate instance specifiers, all tied to error codes like `RdsErrc::kAddressNotAvailable`.

nodes.title: ReadData(std::size_t)

It returns a `Result<ReadDataResult>` object, indicating success or an error code. Errors include `RdsErrrc::kStreamNotConnected` (no active connection) and `RdsErrrc::kInterruptedBySignal` (POSIX_EINTR), where retrying may lead to undefined behavior. The function is not thread-safe and guarantees exception safety. A timeout is provided for non-blocking operation.
